ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wonton entry; no specific code for wonton in chilli oil or black vinegar sauce. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Chinese wonton in chilli oil. A0CKP (filled pasta/dumplings, boiled) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for wonton in chilli oil. No code assigned.

preparation_method: assembled with chilli oil and black vinegar

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of wonton in chilli oil consists of approximately 6 boiled wontons dressed in sauce, weighing around 180 g.

nutrient_source.title: Wonton, boiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / adapted composite estimate for chilli oil wonton
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central; supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Sichuan-style wonton
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for wonton in black vinegar and chilli oil exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for boiled pork wonton (FDC ID 168927, 'Wonton, pork, boiled') and adjusted upward for fat and energy to account for chilli oil dressing (~10 g chilli oil per serving). Sodium is elevated to reflect soy sauce and black vinegar seasoning. Values represent a reasonable composite estimate and are flagged as medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Wontons, boiled — analogous estimate based on 'Pasta, boiled' and 'Dumpling, wheat-based, boiled' GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for wonton in chilli oil. GI estimated at 46 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for boiled wheat-based dumplings and pasta (GI range 40–50 for boiled wheat pasta and dumpling wrappers). The chilli oil and vinegar dressing does not contribute glycemic carbohydrates and may modestly lower postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (46 × 36.9) / 100 ≈ 17.0, using 180 g serving with 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, black vinegar, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g, approximately 936 mg per serving), which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat-based wonton wrappers
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions, as the wheat-based wonton wrappers contribute a moderate glycemic load (GL ~17 per serving); the chilli oil and black vinegar dressing may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response, but portion control remains essential.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from chilli oil dressing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to its elevated sodium content and moderate fat contribution from chilli oil; occasional consumption in small portions is preferable, and the dish should not be a dietary staple for those managing cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wonton in Black Vinegar and Chilli Oil (红油抄手, hóng yóu chāo shǒu) is a classic Sichuan Chinese dish consisting of boiled pork-filled wontons dressed in a bold, aromatic sauce made from chilli oil, black vinegar, sesame oil, soy sauce, garlic, and spring onion. The wontons are made from thin wheat-flour wrappers encasing a seasoned pork mince filling, then boiled until tender and silky. The dish is characterised by its complex flavour profile — simultaneously spicy, tangy, savoury, and nutty — with a slippery, delicate texture from the wonton skin contrasting with the juicy filling. Originating in Sichuan province, this dish is widely popular across Chinese-speaking communities and has become a signature item at Taiwanese-origin restaurant chains such as Din Tai Fung in Singapore. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~195 kcal per 100 g), a reasonable protein contribution from the pork filling (~8.5 g per 100 g), and elevated fat from the chilli oil dressing (~9.2 g per 100 g). Sodium content is high (~520 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and black vinegar seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 46 (low), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 17. Clinically, portion control is advised for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for wonton in black vinegar and chilli oil was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central boiled pork wonton data with adjustments for chilli oil dressing. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for boiled wheat-based dumplings. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation required.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168927, boiled pork wonton) with upward adjustment for fat and energy to account for chilli oil dressing (~10 g chilli oil per 180 g serving). Sodium is elevated to reflect soy sauce and black vinegar seasoning typical of this dish. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero for this type of boiled dumpling product with plant-based oil dressing; no hydrogenated fats are used. GI is an analogous estimate at 46 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for boiled wheat-based dumplings and pasta. The dish is served at Din Tai Fung (Marina Bay Link Mall, Singapore) as sourced from field survey data. Halal status is not confirmed for this restaurant item. The food_group is retained as Grains and staples consistent with the wheat-wrapper-dominant composition.
